processes.title: Company-Level Planning & Goals
processes.description: How you set priorities and goals for the company as a whole. Starts with simple weekly priorities everyone knows, evolves into formal goal frameworks.

stageFocus.foundation: Top 3 to 5 priorities per month or quarter, that everybody knows by heart, and don't drift into different priorities without realizing and explicitly agreeing to it
stageFocus.first-hires: Quarterly goals that everybody knows by heart, made with the team and evaluated afterwards, don't drift into different priorities without realizing and explicitly agreeing to it, and one ambitious growth metric
stageFocus.early-revenue: Cascade company goals to team layers with named owners on key results — imperfect alignment is fine if everyone can see who owns what.
stageFocus.growth: Run planning as a cadence across horizons — top-line metrics from leadership, change goals owned in departments, ~70% local capacity booked. Name who owns the rhythm before you change it.
stageFocus.scaled: Structured annual and quarterly planning cycles. Company OKRs set by leadership. Communicated company-wide. Progress tracked and visible.

processes.title: Team Goals & Cascading Goals
processes.description: How individual teams or departments set their own goals that ladder up to company priorities. Not needed until you have actual departments/teams with different focuses.

stageFocus.foundation: Not needed - you are the team.
stageFocus.first-hires: Not needed yet - still one team with shared goals.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Each function (product and tech, commercial, business ops) has quarterly goals that clearly support company goals. Document how they connect.
stageFocus.growth: Department goals mirror company planning: top-line metrics from leadership, local change goals below, and slack in plans for cross-team dependencies.
stageFocus.scaled: Full goal cascade: company → department → team → individual. Clear accountability at each level. Regular alignment checks. Dependencies managed systematically.

processes.title: Internal Communication about Strategy
processes.description: How the team stays aligned on goals, hears about progress, and gives input on direction. Starts with simple weekly syncs, evolves into structured all-hands and feedback loops.

stageFocus.foundation: Weekly standup (15 min). Everyone shares: what I did, what I'm doing, blockers. Quick verbal check on priorities.
stageFocus.first-hires: Tell the strategy story often enough that each person in a small team shares one mental model — osmosis still works here.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Monthly all-hands with progress updates and Q&A. Quarterly planning sessions with team input. Regular retros or feedback sessions on what's working.
stageFocus.growth: After planning cycles, pass strategy and priorities back down — on the same schedule you report up. Assume most people have heard the story once, not once a week.
stageFocus.scaled: Formal all-hands cadence with pre-submitted questions. Town halls by department. Annual surveys plus ongoing feedback channels. Strategic updates communicated clearly.

processes.title: Shareholder & Investor Reporting
processes.description: How you keep investors and shareholders informed. Covers regular updates, board reporting, and investor relations. Only relevant once you have external investors or shareholders.

stageFocus.foundation: Not relevant yet - no external investors.
stageFocus.first-hires: Start a monthly written update before you have investors — score goals honestly and explain what went differently than expected. Widen the same template when angels arrive.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Regular investor updates (monthly). Board meeting prep (deck, pre-read). Basic cap table management. Coordinate with legal on any shareholder matters.
stageFocus.growth: Structured board cadence (monthly or quarterly). Board pack with financials, KPIs, and strategic updates. Investor relations function forming. Legal compliance on reporting obligations.
stageFocus.scaled: Formal investor relations function. Board governance framework. Audit committee, compensation committee. Quarterly reporting cadence. Compliance with shareholder agreement obligations.

stageFocus.first-hires: Monthly investor update by email — KPIs, cash, hiring plan, key wins/losses, what you need help with. Quarterly board meeting with a proper deck (5-10 slides, pre-read 48h ahead). Keep written board minutes — your shareholder agreement requires them. Cap table managed in a real tool (Carta, Pulley, Ledgy) from day one of the round.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Monthly investor email with a KPI dashboard, cash position, plan vs actuals, and asks. Quarterly board meeting with a structured deck: financials, KPIs, plan vs actuals, hiring, strategic initiatives. Annual budget formally approved by the board. Information rights tracked: most term sheets require monthly metrics + audited annual financials. CEO or CFO owns the cadence — don't let it slip.

Board operates like a real board: chair, formal agenda, written minutes circulated, action items tracked. Quarterly board pack (10-20 slides) covers financials, KPIs, strategic initiatives, risk register. Auditor likely appointed by now. CFO owns investor relations as a defined responsibility. Monthly investor updates are standardised and consistent month-over-month.
stageFocus.growth: Formal board cadence (quarterly or 6 meetings/year). Comprehensive board pack circulated 5 working days in advance. Audit committee active and meeting separately. Annual audited financials. Investor relations is a defined function (usually CFO + finance lead). Compliance with information rights, drag-along, ROFR, and other shareholder agreement obligations actively tracked by legal.
stageFocus.scaled: Full board governance: audit committee, compensation committee, sometimes nomination committee. Monthly investor reporting plus a quarterly board cycle. Audited financials. If preparing for IPO or exit, data room hygiene is a year-round discipline, not a fire drill. IR function staffed (head of IR or CFO + analyst). Compliance team partners with legal and finance on shareholder agreement obligations and side letters.

stageFocus.growth: Full quarterly reporting cadence (10-Q or local equivalent). Earnings release and call every quarter, even at this size. IR function in place (often a head of IR reporting to CFO). Audit committee with independent directors. Material non-public information protocols enforced across the company. SOX 404 readiness program (US) or local equivalent. Trading window communications to staff every quarter.
stageFocus.scaled: Full IR operation: earnings calls, investor day, sell-side analyst coverage management, AGM logistics. Regulator filings on cadence: 10-K, 10-Q, 8-K, proxy statement (or local equivalents). Three standing board committees (audit, compensation, nominations) with majority independent directors. SOX 404 controls audited annually. Material non-public information and trading window protocols enforced. Crisis communications playbook for adverse disclosures (earnings miss, security incident, leadership change).

processes.title: Operational Internal Communication Norms
processes.description: The rules and channels for daily operations - what goes in Slack vs email, response time expectations, how teams coordinate. Prevents chaos and dropped balls.

stageFocus.foundation: One main channel (Slack/Discord). Urgent = DM or call. Non-urgent = channel message. Respond same day if you can.
stageFocus.first-hires: Pick channel jobs and three rules: chat for movement, memory in wiki/tickets, codify Slack decisions. One-page norms if remote or ≥3 non-founders.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Document channel guidelines and CS→engineering escalation. Add an incident fast lane and light artifact map — spoken norms break when handoffs multiply.
stageFocus.growth: Aim for mostly formal information routes with bounded informal access — and put a named owner on legal/commercial asks that would otherwise land on the CEO.
stageFocus.scaled: Full communication architecture: channels organized by purpose and team, SLAs tracked and reported, incident communication protocols, on-call systems, integration with ticketing tools.

processes.title: Who Decides What
processes.description: Clarity on decision rights and authority - who can decide what without asking, and when to escalate. The critical shift from founder-decides-everything to empowered teams.

stageFocus.foundation: Founders decide everything together. No framework needed - you're small and aligned.
stageFocus.first-hires: Align founders first (domain owners, founder sync). One visible approval list: hiring, spend above €X, pivots. Everything else — owner consults, decides, and closes.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Clear decision owners per area. Simple escalation rules (budget thresholds, customer impact). Founder gives input, not decisions. Common issue: Decisions get re-litigated when founders weren't in the meeting.
stageFocus.growth: C-level leaders play for the company, not their function — return out-of-domain debates to the domain owner instead of re-litigating them in the executive team.
stageFocus.scaled: Channel founder intuition through process steps — a shower insight becomes a staged test, not a company-wide pivot by Monday.

processes.title: Organizational Design
processes.description: Org chart, reporting lines, and team structure. How you organize people into teams and who reports to whom. Evolves as you grow from flat to layered structure.

stageFocus.foundation: No org chart needed. Everyone reports to founders. Just clarify what each founder owns to avoid gaps.
stageFocus.first-hires: Simple structure: who reports to whom. Make it explicit even if informal. Update as you add people and roles emerge.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Document team structure as functions form (eng, sales, ops). Define manager roles and spans of control. Revisit quarterly as you grow.
stageFocus.growth: Maintain living org chart. Clear reporting lines and team boundaries. Design structure intentionally (functional vs product teams). Plan for middle management layer.
stageFocus.scaled: Org design as strategic tool. Regular reviews: does structure enable strategy? Optimize spans of control, layers, and team composition. Common issue: Structure doesn't match how work actually flows.

processes.title: Process Documentation
processes.description: How you document and share knowledge about how things work - from simple how-to docs to comprehensive process libraries. Prevents knowledge being stuck in people's heads.

stageFocus.foundation: Not needed yet - just talk to each other.
stageFocus.first-hires: Start a simple wiki or shared doc. Document the 3-5 most important processes (how to deploy, how to handle customer issues, etc.).
stageFocus.early-revenue: Process documentation becomes a habit. New processes get documented. Wiki or Notion space is organized and people actually use it. Review quarterly what needs updating.
stageFocus.growth: Process documentation standards: templates, owners, review cycles. Documentation findable and accessible. New hires can self-serve most questions. Continuous improvement culture.
stageFocus.scaled: Comprehensive process library. Documentation maintained actively. Automated reminders for reviews. Process improvement methodology (e.g., Lean, Six Sigma) if relevant. Regular process audits.

processes.title: Cross-Team Coordination & Collaboration
processes.description: How different teams work together and coordinate without bureaucracy or endless meetings. Critical as you grow from single team to multiple departments. Greiner's phases 4-5 issue.

stageFocus.foundation: Not needed - you are one team working on everything together.
stageFocus.first-hires: Not needed yet - still one team, everyone knows what everyone else is doing. Keep it informal.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Teams are forming (eng, sales, ops). Set up basic coordination: weekly sync meeting, shared updates (wiki/Slack), clear handoffs between teams (e.g., sales → onboarding → support). Common issue: Teams work in silos, don't know what others are doing.
stageFocus.growth: Multiple departments need to coordinate without bureaucracy. Define: which decisions need cross-team input vs can be made autonomously? Regular cross-functional syncs (weekly/bi-weekly). Clear escalation when teams conflict. Shared goals that require collaboration. Common issue: Too many meetings OR teams ignore each other and create conflicts.
stageFocus.scaled: Formal coordination mechanisms: cross-functional project teams, clear RACI for multi-team initiatives, regular dependency mapping, collaboration tools and rituals. Balance autonomy with alignment. Common issue: Bureaucracy slows everything down OR too many meetings crisis (endless discussion, not enough decisions).

processes.title: Data & Reporting Infrastructure
processes.description: Company KPI tracking and definition, dashboards, reporting cadence, data quality

stageFocus.foundation: Probably not needed yet — ship first!
stageFocus.first-hires: Pick 3-5 metrics that tell you if the business is working (e.g. MRR, burn, key activation rate). Track them in a spreadsheet and review weekly. Automate population with simple scripts when you've settled on KPIs.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Define your core KPIs as a team. Introduce a data stack if you're not already on one. Key metrics owned by departments. Weekly/monthly reporting cadence. Move from dirty to scalable before growth hits.
stageFocus.growth: Dashboards for entire team, not just leadership. Communicate progress to entire team. Data team or roles beginning to form. Automated dashboards, alerting, and reporting. Single source of truth across all functions.
stageFocus.scaled: Data team or roles. Automated dashboards, alerting, and reporting. Single source of truth across all functions.

processes.title: Cash Flow Management
processes.description: Knowing how much money you have and how long it will last. Starts with a simple spreadsheet, evolves into forecasting and scenario planning.

stageFocus.foundation: Track bank balance weekly. Know your burn rate. Keep runway visible in a simple spreadsheet linked to the business plan in your pitch deck.
stageFocus.first-hires: Monthly cash flow forecast (3-6 months out). Track burn rate and runway. Alert when runway drops below 6 months. Plan for payroll timing.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Rolling 12-month cash forecast. Track against budget monthly. Scenario planning for hiring/spending decisions. Coordinate with fundraising if needed.
stageFocus.growth: Automated cash forecasting with actuals vs. forecast variance analysis. Department budget owners track their burn. Quarterly scenario modeling.
stageFocus.scaled: Integrated cash management: real-time dashboards, automated forecasts, treasury management, working capital optimization.

processes.title: Financial Planning, Budgeting & Analysis
processes.description: Annual and quarterly budgeting process and timeline, revenue and expense forecasting cadence and accuracy, variance analysis and explanations, department budgets, scenario planning, board/investor reporting package preparation and coordination

stageFocus.foundation: Focus on cash flow (see 2.1). Know your monthly burn and when you run out of money. A single spreadsheet is enough linked to the business plan in your pitch deck.
stageFocus.first-hires: Build a simple headcount and cost budget. Track actual spend monthly against what you expected. Know your runway to the nearest month. Review with co-founders monthly.
stageFocus.early-revenue: A financial model you use in management (not just for a deck), synced with accounting software, monthly close, and most important: cash flow forecast visible to full leadership team
stageFocus.growth: Introduce an annual budget with quarterly reforecasting. Assign budget ownership to team leads. Build a basic board reporting pack: actuals, forecast, key metrics, and narrative.
stageFocus.scaled: Full FP&A cycle: annual plan, quarterly reforecast, monthly variance review. Scenario planning for key business risks. Investor reporting package with consistent metrics and commentary.

processes.title: Accounts Receivable (Outgoing Invoices)
processes.description: Invoice generation and delivery timing, collections process and follow-up (relations with ops people at customers), payment terms management, revenue recognition procedures

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet — no customers.
stageFocus.first-hires: Invoice your first customers as soon as the deal is signed. Track payment status in a spreadsheet. Follow up personally if they're late — it's normal to chase at this stage. Build relations with finance ops people at customers so cash doesn't get stuck for admin reasons.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Standardise your invoicing: consistent templates, clear payment terms (30 days net is common), automated reminders. Know your average days to collect and watch it weekly.
stageFocus.growth: AR should be a structured process: invoice generation on milestone/trigger, automated reminders, escalation for overdue accounts. Revenue recognition should match your contract structure.
stageFocus.scaled: Full AR function: automated invoice-to-cash workflow, collections process, revenue recognition policy, integration with your financial systems. DSO (days sales outstanding) tracked and managed.

processes.title: Accounts Payable & Expense Management
processes.description: Invoice receipt and validation, approval workflows, payment scheduling and execution, employee expenses, corporate card management

stageFocus.foundation: Keep expenses simple: a principle-based policy, personal cards and reimbursements, and a lightweight escalation norm when someone is unsure.
stageFocus.first-hires: Agree on approval thesholds, keep payment simple and disciplined on few company credit cards, get an accountant and tolling in place
stageFocus.early-revenue: Define an approval workflow: who approves what amount? Set up corporate cards for regular spenders. Make sure every invoice gets coded to the right cost centre before it's paid.
stageFocus.growth: AP should be a defined process: invoice intake, approval routing by amount/type, scheduled payment runs. Expense policy documented and signed by all employees.
stageFocus.scaled: Automated AP: invoice scanning, approval workflows in your finance system, payment runs scheduled, full audit trail. Expense management integrated with payroll and GL.

processes.title: Financial Administration
processes.description: Day-to-day bookkeeping accuracy, account reconciliation procedures, month-end close process, audit trail and documentation standards, chart of accounts management

stageFocus.foundation: Create a /finance/ folder and set up a chart of accounts with your bookkeeper before the first hire.
stageFocus.first-hires: Run month-end close through your bookkeeper, file loonheffing in /finance/tax/, and keep copies in your company filing — not only in the bookkeeper's portal.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Maintain the management-to-statutory bridge, file annual accounts and tax/WBSO docs, and document financial systems and internal controls before due diligence.
stageFocus.growth: Keep the management-to-statutory bridge current and archive monthly management reports for at least 18 months.
stageFocus.scaled: Full in-house finance team with defined close calendar. Audit trail and documentation standards enforced. Chart of accounts reviewed annually to match business complexity.

processes.title: HR Administration
processes.description: Payroll processing, benefits administration, time-off tracking and approval, sick leave management, contract lifecycle management, labor law compliance (NL: hour registration, vertrouwenspersoon from first hire, works council from 50 FTE), insurance coordination, employee record keeping

stageFocus.foundation: Use a payroll provider before you hire, assign founder IP to the company, and create /hr/ folders before the first contract arrives.
stageFocus.first-hires: File every signed contract in /hr/ with salary addenda, use lawyer-reviewed templates, keep freelance IP assignments on file, and document your vertrouwenspersoon (confidential counsellor) from the first hire.
stageFocus.early-revenue: One owner for HR admin filing, light HR metrics, and contracts kept out of managers' email.
stageFocus.growth: Migrate to an HRIS with one source of truth, and keep HR filing complete enough to answer buyer due diligence in days, not weeks.
stageFocus.scaled: Dedicated HR ops function. Automated workflows for contracts, onboarding, offboarding, and compliance. Regular audits of employee records and benefits. Clear escalation paths for HR issues.

processes.title: Recruitment & Hiring
processes.description: How you find and hire people, from posting on your network to structured interview processes. Quality of early hires makes or breaks a startup.

stageFocus.foundation: Agree on job description in founding team, Network-based hiring. 2-3 informal conversations. Check references (actually call them). Make sure values align and they can do the job.
stageFocus.first-hires: Agree budget and rough salary band before you post; founder or CFO OK on each offer; no manager-sent contracts — use your template via HR or finance.
stageFocus.early-revenue: No job post without budget and management OK on role plus salary band; CFO or COO approves each offer; HR or finance issues the contract from your template.
stageFocus.growth: For senior hires: three interview rounds with different jobs — hiring manager, potential-only reviewer, culture carrier — and no solo veto outside the hiring manager.
stageFocus.scaled: Professional recruiting: ATS system, talent pipelines, employer branding, data-driven hiring decisions, structured onboarding integration.

processes.title: Onboarding
processes.description: Pre-arrival preparation (contracts, equipment, system access), structured first day/week experience, role-specific training, culture introduction, check-in process (e.g., 30-60-90 day)

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet — save the energy for when you hire.
stageFocus.first-hires: Checklist in a handbook, preparation all done beforehand, full agenda for new hire. After every new hire, onboarding docs are left better than they were found.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Write down your onboarding checklist so it doesn't live in one person's head. Add a 30-day check-in to catch issues early. Role-specific ramp plans help new hires get to impact faster.
stageFocus.growth: Onboarding should run without the founder. Create a structured first week with clear milestones, tool access workflows, and culture touchpoints. Assign an onboarding buddy. Track 30-60-90 day progress.
stageFocus.scaled: Onboarding is a system, not an event. Role-specific tracks, automated access provisioning, structured check-ins, and feedback loops to continuously improve the experience.

processes.title: Offboarding
processes.description: Resignation and termination procedures, exit interviews, knowledge transfer protocols, equipment and access revocation, final pay and benefits processing

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet.
stageFocus.first-hires: When someone leaves, make a checklist: revoke access, return equipment, exit interview, knowledge transfer. Don't improvise on the day — even at 5 people, a bad offboarding leaves gaps.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Offboarding checklist covers IT access, HR paperwork, handover documentation, and an exit conversation. Run it every time. Exit interview insights should feed back into how you hire and manage.
stageFocus.growth: Offboarding is a security and knowledge risk. Build a structured process: IT deprovisioning triggered on day 1 of notice, handover plan agreed with manager, exit interview with HR, reference policy clear.
stageFocus.scaled: Automated offboarding workflows triggered by HR system. Clear deprovisioning SLAs for IT. Legal review for sensitive roles. Knowledge transfer templates. Exit data tracked and reported to leadership.

processes.title: Performance Management
processes.description: Performance review cycles, goal-setting methodology (OKRs/KPIs), 1-on-1 meeting cadence and quality, career development paths, compensation reviews, promotion criteria and process

stageFocus.foundation: Not a formal process yet. Just work closely together and give each other honest feedback directly.
stageFocus.first-hires: Have real 1-on-1s — not just status updates. Ask: what's going well, what's hard, what do you need from me? Make feedback a two-way habit before you have managers doing reviews.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Introduce a simple review cycle (e.g. quarterly check-ins, annual review). Connect goals to company OKRs. Define what 'doing well' looks like for each role. Don't surprise people with feedback they've never heard before.
stageFocus.growth: Formal performance review cycle (bi-annual or annual). Managers trained on giving feedback. Compensation reviews tied to performance. Promotion criteria documented. Common issue: managers avoid hard conversations, letting performance issues fester.
stageFocus.scaled: Full performance management system. Structured review cycles, calibration across departments, clear career frameworks, compensation bands. 360 feedback for senior leaders.

processes.title: Employee Satisfaction & Feedback
processes.description: Employee engagement survey cadence and methodology, pulse check mechanisms, feedback collection channels (1-on-1s, retrospectives, anonymous)

stageFocus.foundation: Stay close — informal check-ins, not a survey. Co-founder teams: block a weekly founder-only sync for relationship feedback and alignment; decision-rights work lives in 1.4.
stageFocus.first-hires: Add a simple start/stop/change question to your weekly team sync: what should we start doing, stop doing, or change? Three questions, five minutes, done. Builds the habit of giving feedback before things fester.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Start regular 1-on-1s with everyone. Use them to surface what's working and what isn't before it becomes a problem. A quick monthly pulse question (e.g. how are you feeling about work this week, 1-5?) adds signal without overhead.
stageFocus.growth: Introduce a lightweight engagement survey once or twice a year. Track trends, not just scores. Make sure feedback actually leads to visible action — otherwise people stop responding.
stageFocus.scaled: Structured engagement programme: pulse surveys, annual engagement survey, 1-on-1 cadence enforced across managers, anonymous feedback channels, and a clear process for acting on results.

processes.title: Learning & Development
processes.description: Recommended from 25+ people. Covers: Training budget allocation and approval, skills development programs, leadership development initiatives, external courses/conference attendance process

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet.
stageFocus.first-hires: Not applicable yet — invest in people's growth through direct feedback and stretch assignments, not formal programmes. If they want to learn something through a course and you can afford it, do it.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Not yet a formal process, but make space for it. A learning budget (e.g. €500/year per person) for books, courses, and conferences is enough. No heavy process — just an approval norm and a structured way to store those decisions.
stageFocus.growth: Define a learning budget and approval process. Identify key skill gaps as you scale. Leadership development starts here — managers need to learn how to manage. Track what's being used.
stageFocus.scaled: Skills development tied to role frameworks, leadership programmes, internal training, onboarding integration. Budget tracked and ROI measured.

processes.title: Governance & Corporate
processes.description: Corporate structure and shareholder arrangements, cap table and board governance, standard commercial contract templates (SaaS, services, NDA), legal request intake and external counsel coordination, founder and investment agreements, basic insurance coverage (liability, professional indemnity, D&O for funded companies)

stageFocus.foundation: Get company structure right from day one: co-founder agreement covering equity, vesting, and leaver terms. Use standard templates and a startup-savvy lawyer — not a corporate firm.
stageFocus.first-hires: Keep corporate and commercial filing current. Give Ops or HR leads formal signing authority before they sign contracts on the company's behalf.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Standard commercial contracts (SaaS, NDA) reviewed once and used consistently. Annual accounts properly adopted and filed on time — late filing risks director liability. Lightweight legal intake; D&O insurance if venture-backed.
stageFocus.growth: Governance rhythm: board minutes and reserved matters documented, plus an authority/signing matrix approved by resolution and a contract register. External counsel on retainer; sector compliance (SOC 2, ISO) needs an owner.
stageFocus.scaled: In-house or dedicated firm relationship. Contract lifecycle management. Insurance reviewed annually. Build audit trail if exit or IPO is plausible.

processes.title: Internal & Labour
processes.description: Employment contract templates and execution, freelancer and contractor agreements with IP assignment, signing authority for HR and Ops, settlement agreements (VSO), labour compliance filing. Hiring approval chain (offer and contract issuance): see 3.2 Recruitment & Hiring. Works council and vertrouwenspersoon: see 3.1 HR Administration.

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet — no employees.
stageFocus.first-hires: Lawyer-reviewed employment and freelance templates — employee IP assignment safeguarded, freelance IP plus an indemnity made explicit (wet DBA). Every contract signed and filed day one; NL labour basics — see 3.1 HR Administration.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Contractor template with explicit IP assignment — use consistently, not one-offs. Employment contracts and amendments findable for diligence.
stageFocus.growth: Keep employment terms consistent across hires and matching what's actually paid — file addenda and move to an HRIS. Settlement discipline documented; works council obligations if headcount triggers apply.
stageFocus.scaled: Employment agreements at scale; disputes and settlements in structured register. HRIS migration without losing contract history.

processes.title: Privacy & IP
processes.description: Founder and employee IP assignment to the company, privacy and GDPR compliance (privacy policy, processing register, data processing agreements), trademark and domain protection, content and software licensing

stageFocus.foundation: Every founder assigns IP to the company at incorporation — it surfaces in every future due diligence. Register trademarks and domains early and record their renewal dates.
stageFocus.first-hires: Know whether your edge rests on proprietary IP. If you collect personal data, design privacy from day one — lawful basis, storage, and a DPA with every processor.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Get a DPA in place for every processor of customer data, version your privacy policy, and keep trademarks and domains on a renewal calendar — your privacy setup should survive investor scrutiny.
stageFocus.growth: GDPR documented, not theoretical — processing register current, internal access controls plus security-awareness training. Register trademarks; set explicit AI-use rules (high-risk AI under the AI Act needs expert advice).
stageFocus.scaled: Compliance as standalone function: GDPR, SOC 2, audit trail for exit. IP portfolio and licenses maintained.

processes.title: Office & Facilities
processes.description: Office space management, lease negotiations, office supplies procurement, facilities maintenance, health and safety compliance, remote/hybrid work policies

stageFocus.foundation: If you take office space, agree who is responsible for it: supplies restocked, lease signed by someone, basic health & safety covered. Don't let it be a chaos of 'whose job is this?'
stageFocus.first-hires: Write your work-location policy and a basic employee handbook before the first hire joins. In the Netherlands, hour registration and a written sick leave procedure are legal obligations from day one.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Formalize your work location policy, assign a named facilities owner, and keep your NL compliance in active use — the ad-hoc approach that worked at 2–10 people starts to break here.
stageFocus.growth: Facilities become a real function. Lease management, office capacity planning, health & safety compliance, maintenance contracts. Plan space ahead — don't move in a crisis.
stageFocus.scaled: Full facilities function: multi-site management, lease strategy, facilities team, workplace experience design. H&S audited regularly. Business continuity plans for facility disruption.

processes.title: IT Administration & User Support
processes.description: Note: Technical infrastructure and architecture decisions remain with CTO. COO domain focuses on user-facing IT operations. Covers: Device procurement policies and standards (which laptop/screen models to purchase), software and SaaS license management and optimization, user provisioning and deprovisioning workflows, IT support ticket management and helpdesk, baseline security protocols for users (password managers, 2FA), equipment lifecycle management (ordering, setup, return)

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet — just use whatever tools you need. No process required.
stageFocus.first-hires: Set up basics: Google Workspace or Microsoft 365, password manager (1Password or similar), 2FA on everything important. Keep a simple list of who has access to what. Provision and deprovision manually for now.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Define your core tech stack and stick to it — every new tool adds admin overhead. Document how to set up a new employee (accounts, devices, access). Deprovisioning is a security risk: make sure it's part of offboarding.
stageFocus.growth: IT admin needs a proper owner. SaaS license audit (you're probably paying for things you don't use). Helpdesk process defined. Device management policy (MDM) if you have a growing fleet.
stageFocus.scaled: IT as an operations function: MDM, ITSM tooling, SaaS management platform, automated provisioning/deprovisioning tied to HR system. Security baseline enforced (SOC 2 or ISO 27001 if applicable).

processes.title: Vendor & Procurement
processes.description: Formal procurement process recommended from 50+ people. Early stage (10-50 people): Approval thresholds (e.g., >€5K needs CEO approval), budget ownership by department, visibility mechanism (e.g., Slack channel), basic contract review checklist (especially for IP transfer, data processing, and payment terms). Mature stage (50+ people): Vendor selection and approval process, contract negotiation and management, purchase order workflows, vendor relationship management, performance reviews, preferred vendor list maintenance

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet.
stageFocus.first-hires: Not applicable yet — just use your judgment. Some afternoon make a list of recurring costs and how to find these invoices (billing portal of vendor, sent to certain email, etc).
stageFocus.early-revenue: Not a formal process yet, but set a basic approval norm: anything above €X needs a second set of eyes. Track significant contracts (renewal dates, costs) in a simple list.
stageFocus.growth: Define a procurement policy: approval thresholds by amount, standard contract review checklist (IP, data processing, payment terms), preferred vendors for common needs.
stageFocus.scaled: Formal vendor management: procurement workflow, RFP process for large contracts, vendor scorecards, contract lifecycle management. Procurement manager or function in place.

processes.title: Sales Operations
processes.description: CRM management, sales process and methodology documentation, pipeline management and forecasting accuracy, territory and quota assignment, sales compensation and commission calculation, deal approval workflows, quoting/proposal generation process

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet — pre-revenue.
stageFocus.first-hires: Keep a deal log in a simple CRM (HubSpot free tier is fine or a board in Clickup). Track: company, contact, stage, expected close, deal size. Review weekly with founders. Don't let deals live only in someone's head.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Define your sales stages and exit criteria. Make sure the CRM is the source of truth. Build a simple pipeline forecast. Quota and compensation structure documented and understood by reps.
stageFocus.growth: Sales ops becomes a role. CRM hygiene enforced, pipeline reviewed weekly, forecast accuracy tracked. Deal approval workflows for discounts or non-standard terms. Commission calculations automated.
stageFocus.scaled: Full sales ops function: CRM administration, territory and quota management, forecast models, compensation plans, deal desk for complex deals. RevOps alignment between sales, marketing, and CS.

processes.title: Customer Acquisition & Lead Management
processes.description: Lead capture from all channels (website, referrals, marketing, partnerships), lead qualification criteria and scoring, routing and assignment logic, response time SLAs, nurture workflows for different segments

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet — pre-revenue.
stageFocus.first-hires: Capture every lead somewhere (simple CRM or kanban style board). Know where leads come from. Follow up fast — response speed is a competitive advantage when you're small.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Define your lead qualification criteria. Set up routing rules in your CRM. Measure lead-to-close conversion. Response time SLAs defined and monitored.
stageFocus.growth: Lead management is a system. Scoring, routing, nurture sequences, and conversion tracking in place. Marketing and sales have agreed definitions for MQL and SQL. Leakage in the funnel identified and addressed.
stageFocus.scaled: Full demand gen and lead management operation. Multi-channel attribution. ABM for enterprise. Automated nurture workflows by segment. Marketing ops and sales ops aligned on lead data standards.

processes.title: Customer Onboarding & Activation
processes.description: Post-sale handoff from sales to customer success, customer welcome process and kickoff, initial setup and configuration support, time-to-value tracking and optimization, early adoption monitoring, first-value milestone celebration

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet — pre-revenue.
stageFocus.first-hires: Your first customers are your reference cases. Onboard them personally — show up, understand their setup, make sure they succeed. Document what good onboarding looks like so you can replicate it.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Write down your onboarding steps so they don't live in one person's head. Define what 'activated' means (the moment a customer gets their first value). Track time-to-activation and iterate.
stageFocus.growth: Onboarding playbook defined. Sales-to-CS handoff clean and documented. Kickoff call template in place. Health check at day 30. Common issue: onboarding breaks down at scale because it was never written down.
stageFocus.scaled: Onboarding as a product and a process. Self-serve onboarding for lower tiers, high-touch for enterprise. Activation metrics tracked in your data stack. Onboarding feedback loop into product.

processes.title: Customer Health, Engagement, Satisfaction, Support
processes.description: Support ticket management system and workflows, knowledge base maintenance, bug/issue tracking and resolution, customer feedback collection mechanisms. NPS/CSAT measurement cadence and methodology, product feedback aggregation and routing to product team, customer health scoring methodology and tracking, proactive engagement cadence based on segment/tier, regular business reviews with key accounts

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet.
stageFocus.first-hires: Answer every support request personally and fast — this is your best source of product feedback. Track issues in a simple log. Don't let anything fall through the cracks: at this stage one bad experience can define your reputation.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Set up a proper support channel (Intercom, Zendesk, or similar). Define response SLAs and make sure you're hitting them. Start measuring NPS or CSAT — even a simple quarterly email survey is enough. Feed feedback back to the product team systematically.
stageFocus.growth: Customer health scoring in place. Proactive outreach to at-risk accounts before they churn. Tiered support by customer segment. Monthly business reviews with strategic accounts. Escalation process defined.
stageFocus.scaled: Full CS operations: health score dashboards, automated playbooks for different health segments, QBR templates, NPS programme, voice-of-customer feeding into product roadmap. CS team segmented by customer tier.

processes.title: Renewal & Retention
processes.description: Renewal timeline and notification process, churn risk identification and intervention, win-back campaigns for churned customers, contract negotiation for renewals, pricing and packaging optimization based on retention data, renewal forecasting accuracy

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet.
stageFocus.first-hires: Treat every renewal as a sales motion. Know when every contract expires. Don't let renewals sneak up on you — one churned customer is a huge % of your ARR at this stage.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Build a renewal calendar. Start the renewal conversation 90 days before expiry. Identify at-risk accounts early using support tickets, low usage, or NPS signals. Track churn rate as a core metric.
stageFocus.growth: Renewal process is a defined playbook. Renewal forecasting in place. At-risk accounts flagged proactively and escalated. Expansion tracked separately from renewal. Churn reasons logged and analysed.
stageFocus.scaled: Renewal ops as a function. Automated renewal triggers, forecasting integrated with CRM, win-back campaigns for churned accounts, pricing optimisation based on retention data. Churn reported to board.

processes.title: Service Delivery
processes.description: Project intake and scoping process, resource allocation and capacity planning, delivery methodology and quality standards, project status communication to customers, post-delivery review and lessons learned, incident/error management and remediation

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet.
stageFocus.first-hires: Deliver your first projects personally. Document what you did, what worked, what you'd do differently — that's the start of your delivery methodology.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Standardise your delivery approach: project kickoff template, status update cadence, scope change process. Know your capacity and don't over-commit. Post-delivery review becomes a habit.
stageFocus.growth: Delivery ops formalised. Resource allocation and capacity planning in place. Project intake with scoping and sign-off. Quality standards documented. Incident process defined for when things go wrong.
stageFocus.scaled: Full delivery operation. Methodology documented and trained. Utilisation and margin tracked per project. Customer satisfaction measured at project close. Lessons learned fed into methodology.

processes.title: Customer Data & Analytics
processes.description: Customer database management and data quality, interaction tracking across all touchpoints (CRM, support, success), customer analytics and reporting dashboards, churn analysis and cohort tracking, revenue metrics (MRR, ARR, expansion, churn), data-driven customer segmentation

stageFocus.foundation: Not applicable yet.
stageFocus.first-hires: Keep a clean CRM from day one — it's much harder to clean up later. Track key revenue metrics (MRR, ARR, churn) in a spreadsheet. Know your numbers.
stageFocus.early-revenue: Define your core customer metrics and who owns each one. Build a simple dashboard (ChartMogul, Looker Studio, or similar) for MRR, churn, and NPS. Make sure sales, CS, and finance look at the same numbers.
stageFocus.growth: Customer data is a strategic asset. Single source of truth across CRM, support, and product. Cohort analysis and churn analysis run regularly. Expansion revenue tracked separately from new.
stageFocus.scaled: Full customer analytics function. Data warehouse with customer data integrated. Real-time health scores, automated alerts, segmentation for targeting. Data used proactively for retention and growth decisions.

reliability.label: Is it working reliably?
options: 0 - Frequent failures: Often doesn't work or gets forgotten
1 - Inconsistent: Works sometimes, depends on who's doing it
2 - Mostly reliable: Works but requires vigilance
3 - Reliable: Consistently good outcomes, rare issues
4 - Optimized: Proactively improved, metrics tracked, exceeds expectations

ownership.label: Who owns it?
options: 0 - No owner: Falls through cracks or CEO does it
1 - Ad hoc owner: Whoever remembers or cares does it
2 - Named owner: Someone is responsible but it's not their main job
3 - Dedicated owner: Someone owns this as part of their role
4 - Team/function: Multiple people with clear RACI, backup coverage

documentation.label: Is it documented?
options: 0 - Nonexistent: No process, people don't know what to do
1 - Tribal knowledge: Process exists in someone's head
2 - Partial and not updated: Something is written down but incomplete or no longer reflects how it actually works
3 - Written and current: Documented well enough for someone new to follow, and roughly reflects reality
4 - Accessible and maintained: Easy to find, people actually use it, updated when the process changes

automation.label: Is it automated?
options: 0 - Manual: Everything improvised, email-based, or purely manual (no automation)
1 - Spreadsheet reliance: Using generic tools (Excel, Google Sheets, basic forms)
2 - Dedicated software: Using purpose-built tools for this specific process
3 - Connected systems: Tools integrated with other systems, workflows partially automated
4 - Self-service automation: Highly automated with minimal manual intervention, exception-based handling

scalability.label: Can it scale?
options: 0 - Will break immediately: Can't handle current load
1 - At capacity: Works now but can't handle growth
2 - Room to grow: Can handle some more volume without changes
3 - Designed to scale: Can handle 2x-10x with minor tweaks
4 - Built for scale: Designed for 10x+ growth, modular/flexible
